title: The Squatterן¿½s Man
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Come, all ye lads anן¿½ list to me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s left your homes anן¿½ crossed the sea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To try your fortune, bound or free,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All in this golden land.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For twelve long months I had to pace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Humping my swag with a cadging face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sleeping in the bush, like the sable race,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As in my song youן¿½ll understand.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unto this country I did come,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A regular out-and-out new chum.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I then abhorred the sight of rumן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Teetotal was my plan.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But soon I learned to wet one eyeן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Misfortune oft-times made me sigh.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To raise fresh funds I was forced to fly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And be a squatterן¿½s man.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Soon at a station I appeared.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I saw the squatter with his beard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And up to him I boldly steered,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With my swag and billy-can.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I said, ן¿½Kind sir, I want a job!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said he, ן¿½Do you know how to snob
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or can you break in a bucking cob?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whilst my figure he well did scan.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Tis now I want a useful cove
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To stop at home and not to rove.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The scamps go aboutן¿½a regular droveן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I ן¿½spose youן¿½re one of the clan?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Iן¿½ll give tenן¿½ten, sugar anן¿½ tea;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ten bob a week, if youן¿½ll suit me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And very soon I hope youן¿½ll be
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A handy squatterן¿½s man.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½At daylight you must milk the cows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Make butter, cheese, anן¿½ feed the sows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Put on the kettle, the cook arouse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And clean the family shoes.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The stable anן¿½ sheep yard clean out,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And always answer when we shout,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With ן¿½Yes, maן¿½am,ן¿½ andֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No, sir,ן¿½ mind your mouth;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And my youngsters donן¿½t abuse.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You must fetch wood anן¿½ water, bake anן¿½ boil,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Act as butcher when we kill;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The corn anן¿½ taters you must hill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Keep the garden spick and span.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You must not scruple in the rain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To take to market all the grain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Be sure you come sober back again
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To be a squatterן¿½s man.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sent me to an old bark hut,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Inhabited by a greyhound slut,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who put her fangs through my poor fut,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, snarling, off she ran.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So once more Iן¿½m looking for a job,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Without a copper in my fob.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With Ben Hall or Gardiner Iן¿½d rather rob,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than be a squatterן¿½s man.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
